ground_truth: Recursive neural networks are tree-structured models designed to represent the natural recursive and hierarchical structure of language, where words and sub-phrases combine into phrases [page no:16, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf]. In these networks, the representation of each non-terminal node in a parsing tree is determined by the representations of all its children [page no:16, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf]. The model functions by iteratively forming high-level representations from lower-level representations through a compositional function [page no:17, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf][page no:18, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf]. Several variations of the basic model exist:*   **MV-RNN:** This variation represents every word and phrase as both a matrix and a vector [page no:17, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf].*   **Recursive Neural Tensor Network (RNTN):** This version introduces more interaction between input vectors using a tensor that defines multiple bilinear forms [page no:17, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf].*   **Tree-structured LSTMs:** LSTM units have been applied to tree structures to avoid the gradient vanishing problem [page no:17, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf]. Recursive neural networks are applied to various tasks, including constituency parsing, phrase-level sentiment classification, and the classification of semantic or logical relationships between nominals or sentences [page no:16, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf][page no:17, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf][page no:18, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf].
expected_context_snippets: English-French translation and constituency parsing. V. RECURSIVE NEURAL NETWORKS Recurrent neural networks represent a natural way to model sequences. Arguably, however, language exhibits a natural recursive structure, where words and sub-phrases combine into phrases in a hierarchical manner. Such structure can be represented by a constituency parsing tree. Thus, tree-structured models have been used to better make use of such syntactic interpretations of sentence structure [4]. Speciﬁcally, in a recursive neural network, the representation of each non-terminal node in a parsing tree is determined by the representations of all its children.

[4]) In its original formulation, RNN language generators are typically trained by maximizing the likelihood of each token in the ground-truth sequence given the current hidden state and the previous tokens. Termed “teacher forcing”, this training scheme provides the real sequence preﬁx to the generator during each generation (loss evaluation) step. At test time, however, ground-truth tokens are then replaced by a token generated by the model itself. This discrepancy between training and inference,

Applications One natural application of recursive neural networks is parsing [10]. A scoring function is deﬁned on the phrase representation to calculate the plausibility of that phrase. Beam search is usually applied for searching the best tree. The model is trained with the max-margin objective [117]. Based on recursive neural networks and the parsing tree, Socher et al. A. Basic model: In this section, we describe the basic structure of recursive neural networks. As shown in Fig. 18a and 18b, the network g deﬁnes a compositional function on the representations of phrases or words ( b,c or a,p1) to compute the representation of a higher-level phrase ( p1 or p2). The representations of all nodes take the same form. Socher et al. [4] described multiple variations of this model. In its simplest form, g is deﬁned as: p1 = tanh(W[bc]),p2 = tanh(W[ap1]) (25) in which the representation for each node is a d-dimensional vector and W ∈RD×2D. Another variation is the MV-RNN [116]. The idea is to represent every word and phrase as both a matrix and a vector. When two constituents are combined, the matrix of one is multiplied with the vector of the other: p1 = tanh(W[CbBc]),P1 = tanh(WM[BC]) (26) in which b,c,p 1 ∈RD, B,C,P 1 ∈RD×D, and WM ∈RD×2D. Compared to the vanilla form, MV-RNN parameterizes the

with the max-margin objective [117]. Based on recursive neural networks and the parsing tree, Socher et al. [4] proposed a phrase-level sentiment analysis framework (Fig. 19), where each node in the parsing tree can be assigned a sentiment label. Socher et al. [116] classiﬁed semantic relationships such as cause-effect or topic-message between nominals in a sentence by building a single compositional semantics for the minimal constituent including both terms. Bowman et al. [118] proposed to classify the logical relationship between sentences with recursive neural networks. The representations for both sentences are fed to another neural network for relationship classiﬁcation. They show that both vanilla and tensor versions of the recursive unit performed competitively in a textual entailment dataset. ground_truth: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are utilized in natural language processing (NLP) for tasks such as sentence modeling, classification, and semantic matching [page no:7, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf][page no:8, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf][page no:9, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf]. **Architecture and Mechanism** In a basic CNN for sentence modeling, each word in a sentence is represented by a word embedding $w_i$ of dimension $d$, and a sentence of $n$ words is represented as an embedding matrix $W /in /mathbb{R}^{n /times d}$ [page no:7, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf]. The network performs convolution by applying a filter $k$ to a window of $h$ words to produce a new feature $c_i$ using a non-linear activation function and a bias term [page no:7, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf]. This process captures contextual features at the word n-gram level by considering a temporal context window [page no:9, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf]. Convolutional kernels essentially act as specific n-gram feature detectors for a target task [page no:8, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf]. These salient n-grams are discovered through convolution and max-pooling layers, which are then aggregated to form an overall sentence vector [page no:9, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf].**Applications** CNNs have been applied to several NLP domains:*   **Sentence Classification:** They are used for tasks including sentiment analysis, subjectivity classification, and question type classification [page no:8, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf]. *   **Information Retrieval:** CNNs can project queries and documents into a fixed-dimension semantic space, allowing them to be ranked based on cosine similarity [page no:9, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf]. *   **Question Answering (QA):** CNNs measure the semantic similarity between questions and knowledge base entries or model optimal representations of question and answer sentences [page no:9, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf]. Variations include the multi-column CNN (MCCNN), which uses multiple column networks to extract information from different aspects like answer types and context [page no:9, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf].**Limitations** A primary shortcoming of CNN architectures in NLP is their inability to model long-distance dependencies [page no:8, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf].

The model attempted to extract rich contextual structures in a query or a document by considering a temporal context window in a word sequence. This captured the contextual features at the word n-gram level. [49], Kim [50], which led to a huge proliferation of CNN-based networks in the succeeding literature. Below, we describe the working of a simple CNN-based sentence modeling network: A. Basic CNN Sentence Modeling: For each sentence, let wi ∈Rd represent the word embedding for the ith word in the sentence, where d is the dimension of the word embedding. Given that a sentence has n words, the sentence can now be represented as an embedding matrix W ∈Rn×d. Fig. 6 depicts such a sentence as an input to the CNN framework. Let wi:i+j refer to the concatenation of vectors wi,wi+1,...wj. Convolution is performed on this input embedding layer. It involves a ﬁlter k ∈Rhd which is applied to a window of h words to produce a new feature. For example, a feature ci is generated using the window of words wi:i+h−1 by ci = f(wi:i+h−1.kT + b) (9) Here, b ∈R is the bias term and f is a non-linear activation function, for example the hyperbolic tangent. B. Applications: In this section, we present some of the crucial works that employed CNNs on NLP tasks to set state-of-the-art benchmarks in their respective times. Kim [50] explored using the above architecture for a variety of sentence classiﬁcation tasks, including sentiment, subjectivity and question type classiﬁcation, showing competitive results. This work was quickly adapted by researchers given its simple yet effective network. After training for a speciﬁc task, the randomly initialized convolutional kernels became speciﬁc n-gram feature detectors that were useful for that target task (Fig. 7). This simple network, however, had many shortcomings with the CNN’s inability to model long distance dependencies standing as the main issue.

MCCNN used multiple column networks to extract information from aspects comprising answer types and context from the input questions. ground_truth: Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) are a class of generative models consisting of two competing networks: a generative neural network and a discriminative network [page no:20, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf]. The generative network decodes latent representations into data instances, while the discriminative network is simultaneously trained to distinguish between instances from the true data distribution and synthesized instances produced by the generator [page no:20, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf]. GANs do not explicitly represent the true data distribution p(x) [page no:20, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf]. In text generation, GAN frameworks may employ an LSTM as a generator and a CNN as a binary sentence classifier to discriminate between real and generated samples [page no:20, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf]. A challenge when applying GANs to text is that gradients from the discriminator cannot properly back-propagate through discrete variables [page no:20, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf]. This issue can be addressed by making word predictions 'soft' at the word embedding space or by modeling the generator as a stochastic policy [page no:20, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf]. When using a stochastic policy, the reward signal from the discriminator is passed back to intermediate state-action steps using Monte Carlo search [page no:20, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf]. The quality of these generative models can be evaluated by measuring BLEU scores of samples on unseen test data [page no:20, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf].

The evaluation of deep generative models has been challenging. For text, it is possible to create oracle training data from a ﬁxed set of grammars and then evaluate generative models based on whether (or how well) the generated samples agree with the predeﬁned grammar [137]. Another strategy is to evaluate BLEU scores of samples on a large amount of unseen test data. The ability to generate similar sentences to unseen real data is considered a measurement of quality [136]. VII. MEMORY-AUGMENTED NETWORKS The attention mechanism stores a series of hidden vectors of the encoder, which the decoder is allowed to access during the generation of each token. Here, the hidden vectors of the encoder can be seen as entries of the model’s “internal memory”.

ground_truth: Parsing consists of two types: dependency parsing, which connects individual words with their relations, and constituency parsing, which iteratively breaks text into sub-phrases [page no:21, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf]. Language exhibits a natural recursive structure represented by a constituency parsing tree, where words and sub-phrases combine into phrases in a hierarchical manner [page no:16, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf]. In practical application, a parser makes a series of decisions to read words sequentially from a buffer and combine them incrementally into a syntactic structure [page no:21, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf]. Transition-based methods are a popular choice for this task because they are linear in the length of the sentence [page no:21, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf]. Additionally, the syntactical parsing problem can be treated as a sequence-to-sequence learning task by uniquely representing the parsing tree with a linear sequence of labels [page no:15, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf][page no:21, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf].

user_input: What is LSTM and how it's different from regular neural networks?
ground_truth: Long short-term memory (LSTM) is a variant of Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) that is widely used in natural language processing (NLP) applications [page no:11, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf]. LSTMs differ from regular neural networks in the following ways: *   **Overcoming RNN Limitations:** Unlike simple RNN networks, which suffer from the 'infamous vanishing gradient problem' that makes it difficult to learn and tune parameters in earlier layers, LSTMs were specifically designed to overcome this limitation [page no:11, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf]. *   **Architectural Hierarchy:** In the evolution of neural network architectures, feed-forward neural networks were superseded by RNNs, which were later superseded by LSTMs [page no:13, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf].  *   **Gate Dynamics:** LSTMs utilize gates that can capture complex linguistic dynamics, such as the reversal effect of the word 'not' in negation phrases [page no:13, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf]. *   **Encoding Capabilities:** LSTMs can be used to encode entire sequences, such as tweets, into hidden states for sentiment polarity prediction or into fixed-size vectors for semantic matching in dialogue systems [page no:13, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf]. Additionally, pre-training a sentence encoder using an LSTM on a large unsupervised corpus has been shown to yield better accuracy than only pre-training word embeddings [page no:19, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf].
expected_context_snippets: sequences with long range context structures. He also proposed deep RNNs where multiple layers of hidden states were used to enhance the modeling. This work established the usage of RNNs on tasks beyond the context of NLP. Later, Sundermeyer et al. [95] compared the gain obtained by replacing a feed-forward neural network with an RNN when conditioning the prediction of a word on the words ahead. In their work, they proposed a typical hierarchy in neural network architectures where feed-forward neural networks gave considerable improvement over traditional count-based language models, which in turn were superseded by RNNs and later by LSTMs. An important point that they mentioned was the applicability of their conclusions to a variety of other tasks such as statistical machine translation [96]. 2) RNN for sentence-level classiﬁcation: Wang et al. [25] proposed encoding entire tweets with LSTM, whose hidden

NLP, xt typically comprises of one-hot encodings or embeddings. At times, they can also be abstract representations of textual content. ot illustrates the output of the network which is also often subjected to non-linearity, especially when the network contains further layers downstream. The hidden state of the RNN is typically considered to be its most crucial element. As stated before, it can be considered as the network’s memory element that accumulates information from other time steps. In practice, however, these simple RNN networks suffer from the infamous vanishing gradient problem, which makes it really hard to learn and tune the parameters of the earlier layers in the network. This limitation was overcome by various networks such as long short-term memory (LSTM), gated recurrent units (GRUs), and residual networks (ResNets), where the ﬁrst two are the most used RNN variants in NLP applications.

conclusions to a variety of other tasks such as statistical machine translation [96]. 2) RNN for sentence-level classiﬁcation: Wang et al. [25] proposed encoding entire tweets with LSTM, whose hidden state is used for predicting sentiment polarity. This simple strategy proved competitive to the more complex DCNN structure by Kalchbrenner et al. [49] designed to endow CNN models with ability to capture long-term dependencies. In a special case studying negation phrase, the authors also showed that the dynamics of LSTM gates can capture the reversal effect of the word not. Similar to CNN, the hidden state of an RNN can also be used for semantic matching between texts. In dialogue systems, Lowe et al. [97] proposed to match a message with candidate responses with Dual-LSTM, which encodes both as ﬁxed-size vectors and then measure their inner product as the basis to rank candidate responses.

They showed that pre-training the sentence encoder on a large unsupervised corpus yielded better accuracy than only pre-training word embeddings. Also, predicting the next token turned out to be a worse auxiliary objective than reconstructing the sentence itself, as the LSTM hidden state was only responsible for a rather short-term objective. C. Deep generative models: Recent success in generating realistic images has driven a series of efforts on applying deep generative models to text data. The promise of such research is to discover rich structure in natural language while generating realistic sentences from a latent code space. In this section, we review recent research on achieving this goal with variational autoencoders (V AEs) [132] and generative adversarial networks (GANs) [128]. LSTM LSTM linear linear LSTM μ σ

user_input: How does a CNN differ from a GAN?
ground_truth: A Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) differ in their fundamental architecture, objectives, and roles: *   **Architecture and Components:** A CNN is a model that utilizes convolution and max-pooling layers to extract salient n-gram features and map input to a fixed-dimensional sentence representation [page no:8, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf][page no:9, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf]. In contrast, a GAN is a class of generative model consisting of two competing networks: a generative neural network that decodes latent representations into data instances and a discriminative network that learns to distinguish between true data and synthesized instances [page no:20, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf]. *   **Objectives:** CNNs aim to extract local features and important n-grams, providing a degree of invariance through local connectivity and weight sharing [page no:11, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf][page no:10, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf]. GANs are designed for adversarial training where the generator attempts to produce realistic instances while the discriminator identifies them as real or synthesized; notably, GANs do not explicitly represent the true data distribution p(x) [page no:20, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf]. *   **Functional Relationship:** While they are different types of models, a CNN can function as a component within a GAN framework [page no:20, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf]. For example, in certain text generation frameworks, a CNN acts as the binary sentence classifier (discriminator) to judge the output of a generator, such as an LSTM [page no:20, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf]. *   **Limitations:** CNNs are often restricted by local sensitivity to word order and typically ignore long-term dependencies [page no:11, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf]. GANs face specific challenges in text generation because gradients from the discriminator cannot properly back-propagate through discrete variables, requiring solutions like 'soft' word predictions or modeling the generator as a stochastic policy [page no:20, source: docs/ml_paper1.pdf].

In [134], this problem was solved by making the word prediction at every time “soft” at the word embedding space. Yu et al. [136] proposed to bypass this problem by modeling the generator as a stochastic policy. The reward signal came from the GAN discriminator judged on a complete sequence, and was passed back to the intermediate state-action steps using Monte Carlo search.

performance over RNNs [88]. Both CNNs and RNNs have different objectives when modeling a sentence. While RNNs try to create a composition of an arbitrarily long sentence along with unbounded context, CNNs try to extract the most important n-grams. Although they prove an effective way to capture n-gram features, which is approximately sufﬁcient in certain sentence classiﬁcation tasks, their sensitivity to word order is restricted locally and long-term dependencies are typically ignored. Yin et al. [89] provided interesting insights on the comparative performance between RNNs and CNNs. After testing on multiple NLP tasks that included sentiment classiﬁcation, QA, and POS tagging, they concluded that there is no clear winner: the performance of each network depends on the global semantics required by the task itself. Below, we discuss some of the RNN models extensively used in the literature. B. RNN models

Fig. 6: CNN modeling on text (Figure source: Zhang and Wallace [51]) Firstly, max pooling provides a ﬁxed-length output which is generally required for classiﬁcation. Thus, regardless the size ofthe ﬁlters, max pooling always maps the input to a ﬁxed dimension of outputs. Secondly, it reduces the output’s dimensionality while keeping the most salient n-gram features across the whole sentence. This is done in a translation invariant manner where each ﬁlter is now able to extract a particular feature (e.g., negations) from anywhere in the sentence and add it to the ﬁnal sentence representation. The word embeddings can be initialized randomly or pre-trained on a large unlabeled corpora (as in Section II). The latter option is sometimes found beneﬁcial to performance, especially when the amount of labeled data is limited [50]. This combination of convolution layer followed by max pooling is often stacked to create deep CNN networks. These sequential

The evaluation of deep generative models has been challenging. For text, it is possible to create oracle training data from a ﬁxed set of grammars and then evaluate generative models based on whether (or how well) the generated samples agree with the predeﬁned grammar [137]. Another strategy is to evaluate BLEU scores of samples on a large amount of unseen test data. The ability to generate similar sentences to unseen real data is considered a measurement of quality [136]. VII. M EMORY-AUGMENTED NETWORKS The attention mechanism stores a series of hidden vectors of the encoder, which the decoder is allowed to access during the generation of each token. Here, the hidden vectors of the encoder can be seen as entries of the model’s “internal memory”.
